description:
You are a deployment engineer who has just rolled-out an ​updated binary of the
    coreutils package on an application server.  
    Before you can announce the change to the rest of the team you must
    produce and archive a **repeatable performance benchmark** that contrasts the
    (single-threaded) and (multi-threaded) behaviour of the new
    “sort” utility on two reference data-sets that are already present on
    the machine.

    Available reference data
    ------------------------
    1.  /home/user/benchmark_data/small.txt   (≈1 KiB)
    2.  /home/user/benchmark_data/large.txt   (≈17 KiB)

    Benchmark requirements
    ----------------------
    • Run exactly four independent timing trials with the _updated_ “sort”
      binary, in the following order:

         Run-ID 1 : dataset=small   parallelism=single
         Run-ID 2 : dataset=small   parallelism=parallel
         Run-ID 3 : dataset=large   parallelism=single
         Run-ID 4 : dataset=large   parallelism=parallel

      – “single” means plain invocation of sort with no extra
        threading parameters.  
      – “parallel” means the same command line but with the additional
        option “--parallel=4”.  
        (The option is accepted by any recent GNU sort; if the container has
        been built correctly it will work without root privileges.)

    • Each timing trial must capture **wall-clock elapsed seconds only**,
      obtained with “/usr/bin/time -f %E” *or* “/usr/bin/time -f %e”
      (either format is permissible – capital or lower-case E).  
      You may redirect the sorted output to “/dev/null” or to a temporary file;
      the decisive factor is measuring the CPU cost, not preserving the
      resulting data.

    Log-file specification
    ----------------------
    When finished, create a single text file called

        /home/user/benchmarks/update_benchmark.log

    that contains exactly **four lines**, **one JSON object per line**, in the
    precise order of the Run-IDs shown above.  The JSON object for each line
    must have precisely these five keys and **in this order**:

       1. run_id          (integer: 1-4)
       2. dataset         (string: “small” or “large”)
       3. parallelism     (string: “single” or “parallel”)
       4. elapsed_seconds (string: the raw string produced by “time”,
                           e.g. “0:01.23” or “1.23”)
       5. exit_status     (integer: the shell exit status of the sort command)

    Example **format** (values will differ; do not copy literally):

        {"run_id":1,"dataset":"small","parallelism":"single","elapsed_seconds":"0.42","exit_status":0}
        {"run_id":2,"dataset":"small","parallelism":"parallel","elapsed_seconds":"0.18","exit_status":0}
        {"run_id":3,"dataset":"large","parallelism":"single","elapsed_seconds":"3.03","exit_status":0}
        {"run_id":4,"dataset":"large","parallelism":"parallel","elapsed_seconds":"1.25","exit_status":0}

    Important formatting rules that *will* be automatically validated:

      • Exactly four newline-terminated lines, no blank lines before, between or
        after them.

      • Every line is valid JSON and **contains only the five keys** shown above,
        in the exact order given above (the test harness uses a strict parser).

      • All string values are double-quoted.

      • exit_status for every line must be 0.

      • elapsed_seconds may be either “M:SS.ss”, “H:MM:SS.ss” or “S.ss”
        (whatever “/usr/bin/time” produces); it is treated as an opaque string.

    Directory & permission notes
    ----------------------------
    • The directory /home/user/benchmarks already exists and is writable by the
      regular ‘user’.  
    • You do **not** need (and will not have) root privileges.  
    • Refrain from installing additional packages; coreutils already
      provides everything required.

    Deliverable
    -----------
    A correctly formatted file  
    /home/user/benchmarks/update_benchmark.log  
    which complies with the specification above.  Nothing else needs to be
    submitted once the log is in place.
truth:
<!--
        Ground-truth data that the automated grader will rely on.
        The grader accepts variable run times, therefore the elapsed_seconds
        field is validated with a regular expression “^([0-9]+:)?[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$”.
    -->

    <!-- Existing directories -->
    <dir path="/home/user/benchmark_data" />
    <dir path="/home/user/benchmarks"   />

    <!-- Reference data files that are present when the scenario starts -->
    <file path="/home/user/benchmark_data/small.txt"><![CDATA[
357
91
782
14
601

439

7

123
    ]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/benchmark_data/large.txt"><![CDATA[
824

470
<!-- (the file actually contains 1000 numeric lines in an arbitrary, fixed order;
      only the first 50 are shown here for brevity) -->
    ]]></file>

    <!--
        Deliverable file name and mandatory structure:
        Each line must satisfy the regex:

          ^\{"run_id":[1-4],"dataset":"(small|large)","parallelism":"(single|parallel)","elapsed_seconds":"([0-9]+:)?[0-9]+\.[0-9]+","exit_status":0\}$

        Lines must appear in the exact sequence of run_id 1,2,3,4.
    -->
    <deliverable path="/home/user/benchmarks/update_benchmark.log"
                 regex_line="^\{\"run_id\":[1-4],\"dataset\":\"(small|large)\",\"parallelism\":\"(single|parallel)\",\"elapsed_seconds\":\"([0-9]+:)?[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\",\"exit_status\":0\}$"
                 lines="4" />
